sections.intro: Frontend system design is the #1 topic that separates senior engineers from mid-level. These questions test your ability to architect entire applications.

cards.title: How to Approach Any Frontend System Design
cards.contentHtml:
The Framework (use this for every question)

1. Requirements (5 min): Clarify functional requirements (what does it DO?), non-functional requirements (performance, scale, accessibility), constraints (browser support, offline, i18n).

2. High-Level Architecture (10 min): Component hierarchy, data flow, state management strategy, API design, routing structure.

3. Deep Dive (20 min): Pick 2-3 areas to go deep on based on the interviewer's interest. Performance optimizations, caching strategy, error handling, accessibility, real-time updates.

4. Trade-offs (5 min): Discuss alternatives you considered and why you chose your approach. Show you understand the trade-offs.

System design interviews are NOT about the 'right answer.' They're about your thought process: how you break down problems, consider trade-offs, and communicate decisions. Talk out loud, ask questions, draw diagrams.

cards.title: Component Architecture Patterns
cards.contentHtml:
Atomic Design

Atoms: Button, Input, Label — smallest units. Molecules: SearchBar (Input + Button), FormField (Label + Input + Error). Organisms: Header (Logo + Nav + SearchBar), ProductCard. Templates: Page layouts with placeholder content. Pages: Templates with real data.

Feature-Based Architecture

src/
├── features/
│   ├── auth/           # Everything for auth
│   │   ├── components/ # LoginForm, SignupForm
│   │   ├── hooks/      # useAuth, useSession
│   │   ├── api/        # auth API functions
│   │   └── types.ts
│   ├── products/
│   └── cart/
├── shared/             # Cross-feature reusables
│   ├── ui/             # Button, Modal, Input
│   ├── hooks/          # useDebounce, useLocalStorage
│   └── utils/          # formatDate, cn()
└── app/                # Routes/pages

cards.title: Design System Design
cards.contentHtml:
When asked to design a design system (Meta, Airbnb, Google-style question):

Core Layers

1. Design Tokens: Colors, spacing, typography, shadows, borders as CSS variables or JS constants. Single source of truth. Support theming (light/dark, brand variants).

2. Primitive Components: Button, Input, Select, Checkbox, Radio, Toggle, Badge, Avatar, Tooltip. Zero business logic. Maximum accessibility. Composable via props (variant, size, disabled).

3. Composite Components: Dialog, Dropdown, Tabs, Accordion, Toast, Pagination — built from primitives. Manage their own state (open/close, active tab).

4. Pattern Library: Authentication forms, data tables, navigation patterns — built from composites. Include documentation and usage examples.

Technical Decisions

Styling: CSS variables for tokens + CSS Modules or Tailwind for components. Distribution: Monorepo with packages per component, or single package with tree-shaking. Documentation: Storybook for component playground. Versioning: Semantic versioning, changelogs, migration guides. Testing: Visual regression (Chromatic), unit tests, accessibility audit.

cards.title: State Management Architecture
cards.contentHtml:
How to choose and structure state management at scale:

State Categories

Server State: Data from APIs. Use TanStack Query — handles caching, deduplication, background refresh, optimistic updates, pagination, infinite scroll. This is 60-80% of most app state.

Client State: UI state (modals, sidebars, theme). Use Zustand — tiny, simple, no boilerplate. Or React context for infrequent changes (auth, locale).

URL State: Search params, filters, pagination. Use the URL as state — useSearchParams(). Shareable, bookmarkable, back-button works.

Form State: Input values, validation, dirty/touched. Use React Hook Form or Server Actions. Don't put form state in global store.

The biggest mistake in frontend architecture is putting everything in a global store (Redux). Server data belongs in a cache (TanStack Query), URL state belongs in the URL, form state belongs in the form. Only truly global UI state (theme, auth, sidebar) needs a global store.

cards.title: Performance Architecture
cards.contentHtml:
Core Web Vitals Strategy

LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) < 2.5s: Preload hero image, use fetchpriority='high', SSR/SSG for fast HTML, optimize fonts (preload, swap), CDN for static assets.

INP (Interaction to Next Paint) < 200ms: Minimize main-thread work, defer non-critical JS, use startTransition for expensive updates, break long tasks with scheduler.yield().

CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) < 0.1: Always set width/height on images/video, use min-height on dynamic sections, font-display: swap with fallback metrics, avoid inserting content above viewport.

Bundle Optimization

Code Splitting: Route-based (automatic in Next.js), component-based (React.lazy), third-party (dynamic imports). Tree Shaking: Use ESM, avoid barrel files that import everything, check bundle with source-map-explorer. Compression: Brotli > gzip. Lazy Loading: Images below fold, heavy components, third-party scripts.

cards.title: Real-Time Architecture
cards.contentHtml:
Approaches (ranked by complexity)

1. Polling: setInterval + fetch. Simple but wasteful. Good for: dashboards refreshing every 30s. Use TanStack Query's refetchInterval.

2. Long Polling: Request stays open until server has data. Less wasteful, still HTTP. Good for: chat apps with moderate traffic.

3. Server-Sent Events (SSE): One-way stream from server. Simple API, auto-reconnect, works with HTTP/2. Good for: notifications, live feeds, stock tickers.

4. WebSockets: Full-duplex, bidirectional. Most complex but most powerful. Good for: real-time collaboration, gaming, live chat. Libraries: Socket.io, Ably, Pusher.

5. WebRTC: Peer-to-peer. For: video calls, screen sharing, file transfer between browsers.

Start simple. Most 'real-time' features work fine with polling every 5-30 seconds via TanStack Query. Only reach for WebSockets when you truly need sub-second bidirectional communication (collaborative editing, gaming).

cards.title: Micro-Frontends Architecture
cards.contentHtml:
Approaches

1. Module Federation (Webpack/Vite): Share dependencies at runtime between separately deployed apps. Each team deploys independently. Most popular approach.

2. iframe-based: Complete isolation but poor UX (no shared routing, styling challenges). Simplest to implement.

3. Web Components: Framework-agnostic custom elements. Each team uses their preferred framework internally.

4. Route-based composition: Different apps own different routes. Reverse proxy routes to correct app. Simplest at infrastructure level.

5. Build-time integration: NPM packages published by each team, composed in a shell app. Strong typing but requires coordinated deployments.

Shared Concerns

Authentication: Shared JWT/session via cookies. Styling: Shared design tokens (CSS variables). Communication: Custom events, shared state library, URL params. Performance: Shared vendor chunks, deduplication.

cards.title: Security in Frontend Architecture
cards.contentHtml:
XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)

Inject malicious scripts into your page. Prevention: Never use innerHTML with user input, React auto-escapes JSX (but not dangerouslySetInnerHTML), Content Security Policy (CSP) headers, sanitize rich text with DOMPurify.

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery)

Trick user's browser into making unwanted requests. Prevention: SameSite cookies (Lax or Strict), CSRF tokens, verify Origin/Referer headers.

Other Concerns

Clickjacking: X-Frame-Options: DENY header. Dependency attacks: npm audit, lockfiles, Dependabot. Secrets: NEVER expose API keys in client code (use server-side proxy). Auth: httpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookies > localStorage for tokens.

NEVER store auth tokens in localStorage — any XSS vulnerability can steal them. Use httpOnly cookies — JavaScript can't access them, so XSS can't steal them.
BWhat is the cursor property?+

Controls mouse cursor appearance: pointer (clickable), default (arrow), text (I-beam), wait (loading), not-allowed (disabled), grab/grabbing, crosshair, move, resize variants, zoom-in/out, none (hidden). Use appropriate cursors to communicate interactivity.

BWhat are CSS shorthand properties?+

margin: 10px 20px (TB LR). border: 1px solid red. background: url() center/cover no-repeat. font: italic bold 16px/1.5 Arial. flex: 1 0 200px (grow shrink basis). inset: 0 (all sides). place-items: center. Gotcha: shorthands reset omitted sub-properties to initial values.

BHow do you truncate text with ellipsis?+

Single line: overflow: hidden; white-space: nowrap; text-overflow: ellipsis;. Multi-line: display: -webkit-box; -webkit-line-clamp: 3; -webkit-box-orient: vertical; overflow: hidden;. The multi-line approach works in all modern browsers despite the -webkit prefix.

BWhat is the box-shadow property?+

box-shadow: x-offset y-offset blur spread color. Example: 0 4px 12px rgba(0,0,0,0.1). Multiple shadows: comma-separated. inset for inner shadow. filter: drop-shadow() follows element shape (including transparent areas) — better for irregular shapes and PNG/SVG.

BWhat is opacity vs rgba for transparency?+

opacity: 0.5 affects the ENTIRE element INCLUDING children. rgba(0,0,0,0.5) or color-mix(in srgb, black 50%, transparent) only affects that specific property. Use color functions for transparent backgrounds; use opacity only for fading entire elements.

IWhat is the appearance property?+

appearance: none removes ALL native form styling (checkboxes, selects, radios). Lets you build completely custom controls. Warning: you lose native keyboard handling, focus rings, and screen reader behavior — must re-implement for accessibility. Test thoroughly.

IWhat is backdrop-filter?+

backdrop-filter: blur(16px) saturate(180%) applies effects to the area BEHIND the element, creating frosted-glass effects. Element needs semi-transparent background to see through. GPU-composited but can be heavy on mobile devices.

IWhat is text-wrap: balance and pretty?+

text-wrap: balance: distributes text evenly across lines — no more one lonely word on the last line of a heading. text-wrap: pretty: prevents orphaned words at paragraph ends. Both are new (2023-2024) and massively improve typography with zero effort. Apply balance to headings, pretty to body text.

IWhat is accent-color?+

accent-color: #6366f1 — one line themes ALL native form controls: checkboxes, radio buttons, range sliders, progress bars. Automatic contrast text color for checked states. The easiest design polish in CSS. Apply to :root for site-wide theming.

AWhat are modern CSS color spaces (oklch, display-p3)?+

oklch(70% 0.15 250): perceptually uniform — equal lightness steps look equal. Better for generating color scales. color(display-p3 1 0 0): wider gamut colors on supported screens. color-mix(in oklch, red 30%, blue): blends in perceptually accurate space. oklch is the future of CSS color — produces better gradients and predictable lightness.

IWhat is scroll-behavior: smooth?+

Apply to html for smooth scrolling on anchor links and scrollTo(). No JavaScript needed. Programmatic: element.scrollIntoView({ behavior: "smooth" }). Always respect preferences: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { html { scroll-behavior: auto; } }.

IWhat is the multi-column layout?+

column-count: 3 or column-width: 250px creates newspaper-style text columns. column-gap and column-rule for spacing/dividers. break-inside: avoid prevents elements splitting across columns. Use for text-heavy content, not page layout.

qa.question: What is the cursor property?
qa.answerHtml: Controls mouse cursor appearance: pointer (clickable), default (arrow), text (I-beam), wait (loading), not-allowed (disabled), grab/grabbing, crosshair, move, resize variants, zoom-in/out, none (hidden). Use appropriate cursors to communicate interactivity.

sections.intro: The core principles of designing systems that handle massive traffic and data without failing.

cards.title: Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling
cards.contentHtml:
• Vertical Scaling (Scale Up): Adding more power (CPU, RAM) to a single existing machine.
    Pros: Extremely simple, no distributed system complexity.
    Cons: Hard hardware limits, single point of failure (SPOF), requires downtime to upgrade.
  • Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out): Adding more machines into your pool of resources.
    Pros: Infinite scalability, fault tolerance, no hardware limits.
    Cons: Data inconsistency risks, complex routing (Load Balancers needed), difficult state management.

cards.title: Load Balancing
cards.contentHtml:
A load balancer sits between clients and servers, distributing incoming traffic across multiple healthy servers.

  • Algorithms: Round Robin (sequential), Least Connections (send to server with fewest connections), IP Hash (ensures a specific user always hits the same server).
  • Layer 4 vs Layer 7: L4 balances based strictly on IP and TCP ports (fast, dumb). L7 balances based on HTTP headers, URLs, and cookies (slower, smart).
  • Health Checks: The LB actively pings servers. If a server dies, the LB stops sending traffic to it automatically.

cards.title: CAP Theorem
cards.contentHtml:
In a distributed data store, you can only mathematically guarantee exactly two of the following three traits:

  • Consistency: Every read receives the most recent write or an error.
  • Availability: Every request receives a (non-error) response, without the guarantee that it contains the most recent write.
  • Partition Tolerance: The system continues to operate despite an arbitrary number of messages being dropped/delayed by the network between nodes.

Note: Because network partitions (P) are a physical reality of the internet and cannot be avoided, real-world systems must choose between AP (Availability) or CP (Consistency).

sections.intro: Managing state when data becomes too large for a single machine.

cards.title: Relational (SQL) vs Non-Relational (NoSQL)
cards.contentHtml:
• SQL (PostgreSQL, MySQL): Structured schemas, ACID compliance, complex joins. Perfect for highly structured, predictable data where consistency is critical (e.g., Financial transactions). Hard to horizontally scale.
  • NoSQL (MongoDB, DynamoDB, Cassandra): Flexible schemas, eventual consistency, no complex joins. Designed specifically to be horizontally partitioned from day one. Perfect for massive volume, unstructured data, or rapid prototyping (e.g., Social media feeds, IoT sensor logs).

cards.title: Database Scaling Techniques
cards.contentHtml:
• Read Replicas: One "Master" database handles all writes. Multiple "Slave" databases copy the master and handle all reads. Fixes read-heavy bottlenecks.
  • Sharding (Data Partitioning): Splitting a single large database across multiple smaller databases based on a Shard Key (e.g., User ID). If `ID < 1000` goes to DB_A, `ID > 1000` goes to DB_B. Extremely fast, but makes cross-shard joins nearly impossible.
  • Indexing: Creating a specific data structure (like a B-Tree) on a database column to allow O(log n) lookups instead of O(n) full table scans. Speeds up reads massively, but slows down writes (because the index must be updated on every write).

cards.title: Caching Strategies
cards.contentHtml:
Storing expensive computations or database queries in ultra-fast, in-memory stores like Redis or Memcached.

  • Cache-Aside: App queries Cache. If miss, app queries DB, then writes to Cache, then returns to user. Best generic strategy.
  • Write-Through: App writes immediately to Cache AND DB at the same time. Cache is always perfectly consistent with DB. Slower writes.
  • Write-Back: App writes ONLY to Cache and returns success immediately. Cache asynchronously syncs to the DB later. Insanely fast writes, but high risk of absolute data loss if the cache node crashes before syncing.

sections.intro: Common system design interview questions with solution frameworks.

cards.title: Design: Infinite Scroll Feed (Instagram/Twitter)
cards.contentHtml:
Key Challenges

Performance with thousands of items, smooth scrolling, memory management, loading states, error recovery.

Solution Architecture

Virtualization: Only render items visible in viewport (~20 items). Use @tanstack/react-virtual. Items outside viewport are removed from DOM.

Data Fetching: TanStack Query's useInfiniteQuery with cursor-based pagination. Intersection Observer on a sentinel element triggers next page fetch.

Optimistic Updates: Like/unlike with useOptimistic. Show change instantly, revert on error.

Image Loading: Lazy load with loading='lazy', blur placeholder, progressive JPEG/AVIF. Skeleton screens during load.

State: Each post is a cache entry in TanStack Query. Mutations invalidate specific queries. New posts prepended without refetching entire feed.

cards.title: Design: Real-Time Collaborative Editor (Google Docs)
cards.contentHtml:
Key Challenges

Concurrent editing without conflicts, real-time sync, offline support, cursor presence.

Solution Architecture

Conflict Resolution: CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) libraries: Yjs, Automerge. Each client has a local replica that syncs automatically. CRDTs guarantee convergence without central authority.

Transport: WebSocket for real-time sync. Fallback to polling for unreliable connections. Use Y-websocket or Liveblocks.

Editor: ProseMirror, TipTap, or Lexical with CRDT integration. Rich text model maps to CRDT operations.

Presence: Broadcast cursor positions and selections via WebSocket. Debounce position updates (50-100ms).

Offline: CRDT operations stored in IndexedDB. Sync when reconnected. Works because CRDTs handle merge automatically.

cards.title: Design: E-Commerce Product Page (Amazon)
cards.contentHtml:
Key Challenges

Performance with SEO, dynamic pricing/stock, image gallery, reviews, recommendations, cart interaction.

Solution Architecture

Rendering: Partial Prerendering (PPR). Static: layout, product info, image gallery. Dynamic: price, stock, personalized recommendations, cart count. Static parts from CDN in <50ms.

Images: next/image with AVIF, blur placeholder, zoom on hover (separate high-res lazy-loaded). Gallery with virtual scroll for many images.

Cart: Zustand store + optimistic updates. Add-to-cart shows instant feedback. Server confirms async.

Reviews: Paginated, loaded below-fold with Suspense. Sort/filter as URL params (shareable).

SEO: Structured data (Product schema), canonical URLs, generateMetadata for dynamic titles, breadcrumbs.

cards.title: Design: Chat Application (Slack/Discord)
cards.contentHtml:
Key Challenges

Real-time messaging, channels/DMs, message history, typing indicators, file uploads, notifications.

Solution Architecture

Transport: WebSocket (Socket.io or native WS) for real-time messages, typing indicators, presence. REST/Server Actions for: message history, file uploads, channel management.

Message List: Virtualized list (bottom-anchored). Load older messages on scroll up (reverse pagination). Optimistic send: show message immediately with 'pending' state.

State: TanStack Query for message history (cached per channel). Zustand for UI state (active channel, sidebar, compose). WebSocket events update TanStack Query cache directly.

Notifications: Service Worker + Push API for background notifications. Badge count via shared state. Permission request UX.

File Uploads: Presigned URLs to S3/R2. Client uploads directly (no server proxy). Progress bar with XMLHttpRequest or fetch + ReadableStream.

sections.intro: Mastering distributed systems, scalability patterns, and high-level architectural trade-offs for technical lead positions.

cards.title: Micro-Frontend Architectures
cards.contentHtml:
Scaling frontend development across large, decoupled teams.

Module Federation

The industry standard for MFEs. Allows dynamic loading of code from other independently deployed applications at runtime, sharing dependencies to avoid bloat.

Islands Architecture

Popularized by Astro. Sending static HTML with specific "islands" of interactivity (React/Vue/Angular) that hydrate independently. Massive LCP/INP wins.

// Webpack Module Federation example
new ModuleFederationPlugin({
  name: 'app1',
  remotes: {
    app2: 'app2@http://localhost:3002/remoteEntry.js',
  },
  shared: { react: { singleton: true }, 'react-dom': { singleton: true } },
});

cards.title: Real-time & Event-Driven Systems
cards.contentHtml:
Using Event Sourcing and CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) for complex state management.

  • Event Sourcing: Storing the state of an application as a sequence of immutable events rather than just the current state. Enables infinite \"undo\" and historical auditing.
  • Change Data Capture (CDC): Observing database logs to stream changes to downstream consumers (elastic search, caches, other services) in real-time.
  • WebSockets vs Server-Sent Events (SSE): Use WebSockets for full-duplex (chat, games). Use SSE for one-way server-to-client updates (stock tickers, notifications) as it's more lightweight and has native auto-reconnect.

cards.title: Distribution & Availability
cards.contentHtml:
Modern architectures prioritize resilience and global distribution.

  • CAP Theorem: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance. You can only guarantee two in a distributed system during a network partition.
  • PACELC Theorem: An extension of CAP. If there's a partition (P), trade-off between availability (A) and consistency (C). Else (E), trade-off between latency (L) and consistency (C).
  • Global Replication: Active-Active vs Active-Passive. Use Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) or Last-Writer-Wins (LWW) for conflict resolution.

cards.title: Advanced Scaling & Resilience
cards.contentHtml:
• Backpressure: Mechanism to signal a producer that the consumer is overwhelmed. Prevents service crashes under load.
  • Circuit Breaker: Prevents a service from attempting a failing operation, allowing it to recover instead of being hammered by retries.
  • Saga Pattern: Managing data consistency across microservices in distributed transactions via a sequence of local transactions and compensating actions.

sections.intro: 42 interview questions from very basic to very advanced. Click to reveal answers.

qa.question: What is System Design?
qa.answerHtml: The process of defining the architecture, modules, interfaces, and data for a system to satisfy specified requirements. It's about making high-level architectural choices to scale.

qa.question: What is a Load Balancer?
qa.answerHtml: A device or software (like Nginx or HAProxy) that acts as a reverse proxy and distributes network or application traffic across a number of servers to prevent single-point overload.

qa.question: Explain Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling.
qa.answerHtml: Vertical scaling (Scaling Up) means upgrading a single server's hardware. Horizontal scaling (Scaling Out) means adding more independent servers to a pool to share the load.

qa.question: What is a CDN (Content Delivery Network)?
qa.answerHtml: A geographically distributed network of proxy servers and their data centers. The goal is to cache static assets (like images, JS, CSS) closer to users to reduce latency drastically.

qa.question: What is database caching?
qa.answerHtml: Storing frequently accessed data in a fast, in-memory store (like Redis or Memcached) to reduce the number of slow, heavy queries hitting the primary database.

qa.question: What does stateful vs stateless mean?
qa.answerHtml: A stateless architecture means the server does not store any information about the client session (every request must contain all auth info, like JWTs). Stateful means the server remembers the client (like saving a session ID in memory).

qa.question: What is the purpose of a Reverse Proxy?
qa.answerHtml: A server that sits in front of web servers and forwards client requests to them. Used for load balancing, SSL termination, and caching.

qa.question: Explain basically what a Microservice architecture is.
qa.answerHtml: Breaking down a massive monolithic application into small, independent, specialized services that communicate over APIs. Each service can be developed, deployed, and scaled totally independently.

qa.question: What is DNS?
qa.answerHtml: Domain Name System. The phonebook of the internet. It translates human-readable domain names (google.com) into computer-readable IP addresses.

qa.question: What is API Rate Limiting?
qa.answerHtml: Restricting the number of requests a user or IP can make to an API within a specific time window to prevent abuse, scraping, or DDoS attacks.

qa.question: Explain the CAP Theorem.
qa.answerHtml: In a distributed data store, you can only mathematically guarantee two of three traits: Consistency (every read gets the latest write), Availability (every request gets a response), and Partition Tolerance (system works despite network drops). Because of network physics, you must choose CP or AP.

qa.question: What is Database Sharding?
qa.answerHtml: Horizontally partitioning a database. Instead of one massive DB, you split data across multiple databases using a shard key (e.g., users A-M on DB1, N-Z on DB2). It solves pure dataset size limits but makes cross-shard joins impossible.

qa.question: What is the "Thundering Herd" problem in caching?
qa.answerHtml: When a popular cache key expires, thousands of concurrent requests miss the cache simultaneously and instantly slam the primary database, often crashing it. Solution: Use cache locks or add slight random jitter to expiration times.

qa.question: Explain Eventual Consistency vs Strong Consistency.
qa.answerHtml: Strong consistency guarantees that once a write occurs, all subsequent reads instantly reflect it (slow). Eventual consistency allows temporary staleness across replicated nodes, but guarantees they will ultimately synchronize (fast).

qa.question: What is Long Polling vs WebSockets vs Server-Sent Events?
qa.answerHtml: Long polling holds an HTTP connection open until the server has new data. WebSockets provide a true full-duplex, persistent TCP connection for two-way realtime data. Server-Sent Events (SSE) provide a persistent one-way connection from server to client.

qa.question: Explain Consistent Hashing and why it is used.
qa.answerHtml: A hashing technique used in distributed systems (like Memcached clusters). Instead of modulo hashing which requires massive data reshuffling if a node is added/removed, consistent hashing places nodes on a circular hash ring, meaning adding/removing a node only disrupts its immediate neighbor.

qa.question: What is a Message Queue?
qa.answerHtml: An asynchronous service-to-service communication mechanism (like RabbitMQ or Kafka) used to decouple heavy processing. App servers push tasks onto the queue instantly, while worker servers pull tasks off the queue at their own pace without crashing.

qa.question: What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL?
qa.answerHtml: SQL offers strict schemas, complex joins, and ACID compliance (scale vertically). NoSQL offers flexible schemas, sacrifices strong consistency for massive horizontal scalability, and is optimized for specific access patterns like key-value or document storage.

qa.question: What is the Two-Phase Commit (2PC) protocol?
qa.answerHtml: A distributed algorithm that coordinates all processes participating in a distributed atomic transaction on whether to commit or abort (rollback) the transaction. It ensures ACID properties across multiple disparate databases.

qa.question: How would you design a URL shortener like bit.ly?
qa.answerHtml: Requirements: High read-to-write ratio. Tech: Relational DB for strict ACID, or NoSQL for sheer scale. Algorithm: Base62 encoding of a unique auto-incrementing integer ID. Use Memcached heavily to cache the `shortURL -> longURL` mapping since reads outnumber writes 100:1.

qa.question: Explain the difference between L4 and L7 Load Balancing.
qa.answerHtml: L4 (Transport Layer) balances based on IP and TCP/UDP ports; it is fast but cannot see application data. L7 (Application Layer) balances based on HTTP headers, cookies, and URL paths; it is slower but enables intelligent routing (e.g., sending /api to different servers than /static).

qa.question: What is a Conflict-free Replicated Data Type (CRDT)?
qa.answerHtml: A data structure which can be replicated across multiple nodes in a network where replicas can be updated independently and concurrently without coordination, and it is mathematically guaranteed to converge to a consistent state.

qa.question: Explain the "Saga Pattern" for distributed transactions.
qa.answerHtml: A failure management pattern that helps establish consistency in distributed systems. It coordinates transactions between multiple microservices using a sequence of local transactions. If one fails, the Saga executes "compensating transactions" to undo the preceding successful transactions.

qa.question: What is CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)?
qa.answerHtml: An architectural pattern that separates the models for reading and writing data. Commands (writes) use one model/database, while Queries (reads) use another (often a flattened/denormalized read replica). This allows independent scaling of read and write workloads.

qa.question: Explain the concept of "Sidecars" in microservices.
qa.answerHtml: A deployment pattern where a helper container (the sidecar) is attached to the main application container to handle peripheral tasks like logging, monitoring, or service-mesh networking (e.g., Envoy/Istio) without modifying the app code.

qa.question: What is the "Thundering Herd" problem and how do you mitigate it?
qa.answerHtml: When a popular cached item expires, many concurrent requests all trigger a database re-fetch simultaneously. Mitigation: Use "Lease" or "Locking" where only one request updates the cache while others wait or serve slightly stale data.

qa.question: Compare Optimistic vs Pessimistic Locking.
qa.answerHtml: Pessimistic locking assumes conflicts will happen and locks the record before update (blocking). Optimistic locking assumes conflicts are rare; it checks a version/timestamp during the update and fails if it has changed, requiring a retry (non-blocking).

qa.question: What is "Write-Ahead Logging" (WAL)?
qa.answerHtml: A standard technique for providing atomicity and durability in databases. Changes are first written to a persistent log file before being applied to the actual data files. This allows the DB to recover state after a crash.

qa.question: Explain "Fan-out" in messaging systems.
qa.answerHtml: The process of delivering a single message to multiple consumers or queues simultaneously. Commonly used for social media feeds: when a celebrity posts, the message fans out to the feeds of millions of followers.

qa.question: What are Bloom Filters and why use them in System Design?
qa.answerHtml: A space-efficient probabilistic data structure used to test whether an element is a member of a set. It can return "false positive" matches but NEVER "false negatives". Use: checking if a username is taken or if a URL is malicious before hitting a slow DB.

qa.question: What is the "N+1 Query Problem" and how do you solve it?
qa.answerHtml: Happens when an ORM executes one query to fetch parent records, and then N additional queries to fetch children of each parent. Solution: Use "Eager Loading" (SQL Joins or IN clauses) to fetch all required data in 1 or 2 queries.

qa.question: Explain "Backpressure" in distributed streams.
qa.answerHtml: A mechanism that allows a overwhelmed consumer to tell a producer to slow down or stop sending data until the consumer catches up. Essential for stability in systems like Kafka, Akka, or Reactive Streams.

qa.question: What is a "Circuit Breaker" in microservices?
qa.answerHtml: A state machine that prevents a service from calling another service that is likely to fail. States: CLOSED (calls allowed), OPEN (calls blocked for N seconds), and HALF-OPEN (allows a few test calls). Stops "cascading failures" throughout the system.

qa.question: Explain "Shadow DOM" isolation benefits for Design Systems.
qa.answerHtml: Shadow DOM allows for complete CSS and DOM encapsulation. It ensures that styles defined in a design system component (like a button) cannot be accidentally overridden by "global" styles on the host page, and vice versa.

qa.question: What is a SPA vs MPA?
qa.answerHtml: SPA (Single Page Application): One HTML file, JS handles all navigation (client-side routing). Fast transitions but: slow initial load, SEO challenges, complex state. MPA (Multi Page Application): Each page is a new HTML file from server. Simpler, better SEO, but full page reloads. Modern: Next.js/Nuxt combine both — SPA-like transitions with server rendering.

qa.question: What is client-side rendering (CSR) vs server-side rendering (SSR)?
qa.answerHtml: CSR: Browser downloads empty HTML + JS bundle, JS renders everything. Pros: fast subsequent navigation. Cons: slow first load, no SEO for dynamic content. SSR: Server renders HTML for each request. Pros: fast first paint, SEO friendly. Cons: server load per request. Modern: use SSR for initial load, CSR for navigation (hybrid).

qa.question: What is a CDN?
qa.answerHtml: Content Delivery Network — globally distributed servers that cache and serve static assets (HTML, CSS, JS, images) from the nearest location to the user. Reduces latency from 200-500ms to 20-50ms. Use for: static files, pre-rendered pages (SSG/ISR). Providers: Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront, Fastly, Vercel Edge Network.

qa.question: What is lazy loading?
qa.answerHtml: Deferring loading of non-critical resources until they're needed. Images: loading='lazy'. Components: React.lazy() + Suspense. Routes: dynamic import(). Benefits: faster initial load, less bandwidth. Implement with: native lazy loading, Intersection Observer, route-based code splitting.

qa.question: What is responsive design?
qa.answerHtml: Designing for all screen sizes. Techniques: 1) Fluid layouts (%, fr, flex). 2) Media queries (@media). 3) Responsive images (srcset). 4) Mobile-first approach. 5) Modern: container queries, clamp(), auto-fit/auto-fill grid. 6) Viewport units (vh/vw/dvh). The goal: one codebase, every device.

qa.question: What are Core Web Vitals?
qa.answerHtml: Google's metrics for user experience: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) < 2.5s: How fast the main content loads. INP (Interaction to Next Paint) < 200ms: How fast the page responds to user input. CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) < 0.1: How much the layout shifts during load. They directly affect SEO ranking. Measured by Chrome UX Report, Lighthouse, PageSpeed Insights.

qa.question: How would you optimize a website's performance?
qa.answerHtml: Network: CDN, compression (Brotli), HTTP/2, preload/preconnect critical resources. Rendering: SSR/SSG, critical CSS, above-fold optimization, defer non-critical JS. Assets: Image optimization (AVIF/WebP), responsive images, lazy loading, font optimization (swap + preload). Runtime: Code splitting, tree shaking, virtualization for long lists, debounce/throttle, web workers for heavy computation.

qa.question: What is code splitting?
qa.answerHtml: Breaking your JS bundle into smaller chunks loaded on demand. Route-based: Each page is a separate chunk (automatic in Next.js). Component-based: React.lazy(() => import('./Heavy')). Library-based: Dynamic import for heavy libraries (const pdf = await import('pdfjs')). Result: smaller initial bundle = faster load. Analyze with webpack-bundle-analyzer or source-map-explorer.

qa.question: Explain caching strategies.
qa.answerHtml: Cache-First: Check cache before network. Best for: static assets (CSS, JS, images). Network-First: Try network, fallback to cache. Best for: API responses, dynamic content. Stale-While-Revalidate: Serve cached immediately, update cache in background. Best for: content that changes but freshness isn't critical. Cache-Only: Offline-first apps. Network-Only: Always fresh data. Headers: Cache-Control, ETag, Last-Modified.

qa.question: What is tree shaking?
qa.answerHtml: Dead code elimination — removing unused exports from the final bundle. Requires: ES modules (import/export), static analysis at build time. Webpack/Rollup/Turbopack analyze which exports are actually imported and remove the rest. Gotchas: barrel files (index.ts re-exporting everything) can prevent tree shaking — import directly from source files instead.

qa.question: How do you handle authentication in a frontend application?
qa.answerHtml: Token storage: httpOnly cookies (secure, immune to XSS). Never localStorage for tokens. Flow: Login → server sets httpOnly cookie → cookie sent automatically with every request. JWT: Access token (short-lived, 15min) + refresh token (httpOnly cookie, 7 days). OAuth/OIDC: For third-party login (Google, GitHub). Libraries: Auth.js, Clerk, auth0. Server Components: Read session server-side, never expose tokens to client.

qa.question: What is the difference between HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3?
qa.answerHtml: HTTP/1.1: One request per connection, head-of-line blocking, text protocol. HTTP/2: Multiplexing (many requests over one connection), header compression, server push, binary protocol. Domain sharding unnecessary. HTTP/3: QUIC transport (UDP instead of TCP), faster connection setup, no TCP head-of-line blocking. Automatic in most CDNs.

qa.question: How do you handle error boundaries in a frontend app?
qa.answerHtml: Component level: React error boundaries catch rendering errors, show fallback UI, log to error service. Route level: error.tsx in Next.js catches per-route. Global: global-error.tsx for root errors. API errors: Try/catch in Server Actions, error states in useActionState, TanStack Query error states. Monitoring: Sentry, Datadog, LogRocket for production error tracking.

qa.question: What is a Service Worker?
qa.answerHtml: A JavaScript file that runs as a network proxy between your app and the internet. Can: intercept fetch requests, serve cached responses (offline support), receive push notifications, run background sync. Lifecycle: install → activate → fetch. Registered with navigator.serviceWorker.register(). Foundation of Progressive Web Apps (PWAs). Must be served over HTTPS.

qa.question: What is the PRPL pattern?
qa.answerHtml: Push critical resources for initial route (preload). Render initial route ASAP. Pre-cache remaining routes (service worker). Lazy-load remaining routes on demand. Optimizes for first load AND subsequent navigations. Implemented naturally by frameworks like Next.js with SSR + code splitting + prefetching.

qa.question: Design a frontend monitoring and observability system.
qa.answerHtml: Error tracking: Global error handler + error boundaries → Sentry/Datadog. Capture: stack traces, user context, breadcrumbs (actions before error). Performance: Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) via web-vitals library → analytics pipeline. User behavior: Session replay (LogRocket, FullStory) for debugging. Custom metrics: Business KPIs (conversion, engagement) via custom events. Alerting: Error rate spikes, CWV regression, API latency thresholds. Dashboard: Grafana/Datadog for real-time visibility.

qa.question: How would you design the frontend for a design system?
qa.answerHtml: Tokens: CSS variables for colors, spacing, typography, shadows. Components: Primitive (Button, Input) → Composite (Dialog, Dropdown). Each component: accessible by default (ARIA), customizable (variants, sizes via props), documented (Storybook), tested (visual regression + unit). Distribution: Monorepo with per-component packages, tree-shakeable. Theming: CSS variables + data-theme attribute. Versioning: Semver, auto-generated changelogs, codemods for migrations.

qa.question: How would you architect a micro-frontend application?
qa.answerHtml: Composition: Module Federation (runtime dependency sharing) or route-based (different apps for different paths). Shared concerns: Design tokens (CSS variables), auth (shared cookies), routing (shell app controls top-level). Communication: Custom events, URL params, shared state library. Deployment: Independent CI/CD per team. Performance: Shared vendor chunks, deduplication, lazy loading boundaries. Trade-offs: Increased complexity, harder debugging, bundle size coordination. Only use when team autonomy is critical.

qa.question: How do you handle internationalization (i18n) at scale?
qa.answerHtml: Architecture: Locale in URL (/en/page), middleware for detection (Accept-Language + geo + cookie). Translations: JSON files per locale, loaded server-side (Server Components). RTL support: CSS logical properties (margin-inline, padding-block). Formatting: Intl API for dates, numbers, currencies. Content: Translation management system (Crowdin, Phrase) for non-devs. Performance: Only load current locale's strings, lazy-load others. Testing: Pseudo-localization to catch layout issues.

qa.question: Design a real-time collaborative whiteboard.
qa.answerHtml: Canvas: HTML Canvas API or WebGL (better for complex graphics). Sync: CRDT (Yjs) for conflict-free collaborative state. Each stroke/shape is a CRDT operation. Transport: WebSocket for low-latency sync. Performance: Spatial partitioning — only sync/render visible viewport area. Virtualize off-screen content. Offline: IndexedDB for local state, CRDT handles merge on reconnect. History: Undo/redo stack per user via CRDT operations. Presence: Cursor positions + selected elements broadcast at 10-20fps.

qa.question: How do you handle state management at scale?
qa.answerHtml: Classification: Server state (TanStack Query — 70% of most apps), URL state (search params — 10%), client state (Zustand — 15%), form state (React Hook Form — 5%). Don't put everything in one global store. Architecture: Feature-based stores, selectors for derived state, middleware for side effects. Normalization: For relational data, normalize in cache (TanStack Query handles this). Optimistic updates: Show instant feedback, reconcile with server response.

qa.question: Design an accessible data table with sorting, filtering, and pagination.
qa.answerHtml: Semantics: <table>, <thead>, <tbody>, <th scope='col'>. Sort: aria-sort='ascending|descending|none' on <th>, button inside th for click target. Filter: Controlled inputs above table, apply filters as URL params (shareable, bookmarkable). Pagination: role='navigation', aria-label='Pagination', announce page changes with aria-live. Performance: Virtual rows for 1000+ items (@tanstack/react-table + react-virtual). Responsiveness: Horizontal scroll wrapper or card layout on mobile.

qa.question: What is a REST API?
qa.answerHtml: Representational State Transfer — an architectural style for APIs. Principles: stateless requests, resource-based URLs, HTTP methods for actions (GET=read, POST=create, PUT/PATCH=update, DELETE=remove), standard status codes (200, 201, 400, 401, 404, 500). Endpoints: /api/users (collection), /api/users/123 (single resource).

qa.question: What is the difference between GET and POST?
qa.answerHtml: GET: Read data, parameters in URL, cacheable, bookmarkable, idempotent (same result every time), limited URL length. POST: Create/modify data, body contains data, not cached, not bookmarkable, not idempotent, no size limit. Use GET for fetching, POST for mutations.

qa.question: What is CORS?
qa.answerHtml: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — browser security mechanism. Requests to a different origin (protocol + domain + port) are blocked unless the server sends Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers. Preflight requests (OPTIONS) check permission for non-simple requests. Server must explicitly allow cross-origin access.

qa.question: What is progressive enhancement vs graceful degradation?
qa.answerHtml: Progressive enhancement: Start with basic functionality (HTML), add layers (CSS, JS). Works for everyone, enhanced for modern browsers. Graceful degradation: Build full-featured first, add fallbacks for older browsers. PE is preferred — ensures core functionality is always available.

qa.question: What is the difference between a library and a framework?
qa.answerHtml: Library: You call it (React — you decide when/how to render). Framework: It calls you (Angular, Next.js — the framework controls the flow, you fill in the blanks). 'Inversion of control' is the key distinction. Libraries are flexible, frameworks are opinionated.

qa.question: What is a WebSocket?
qa.answerHtml: Full-duplex communication protocol over a single TCP connection. Unlike HTTP (request-response), WebSocket maintains a persistent connection for bidirectional data. Connection: HTTP upgrade request → WebSocket handshake. Use for: real-time chat, live data feeds, collaborative editing, gaming. Events: onopen, onmessage, onclose, onerror.

qa.question: What is a Progressive Web App (PWA)?
qa.answerHtml: Web app with native-like capabilities. Requirements: HTTPS, Service Worker (offline, push notifications, background sync), Web App Manifest (icon, name, splash screen, display mode). Features: installable on home screen, works offline, push notifications. manifest.json + navigator.serviceWorker.register(). Lighthouse audits PWA readiness.

qa.question: How do you handle API errors in a frontend application?
qa.answerHtml: HTTP level: Check response.ok, handle status codes (401→login, 403→forbidden, 404→not found, 429→rate limited, 500→server error). UI: Error boundaries for render errors, toast notifications for transient errors, error pages for fatal errors. Retry: Exponential backoff for transient failures (TanStack Query does this automatically). Logging: Send errors to monitoring service (Sentry).

qa.question: What is optimistic UI?
qa.answerHtml: Update the UI immediately before server confirms the action. If server fails, revert. Makes the app feel instant. Example: liking a post — show the heart immediately, send request in background, revert if it fails. Implemented with: React 19's useOptimistic, TanStack Query's onMutate callback, Zustand with rollback.

qa.question: What is debouncing in the context of search?
qa.answerHtml: Wait until the user stops typing for N ms before sending API request. Without debouncing: each keystroke triggers a request (typing 'react' sends 5 requests). With debouncing (300ms delay): sends 1 request after the user pauses. Implementation: useDebounce hook or useDeferredValue in React.

qa.question: What is A/B testing architecture?
qa.answerHtml: Server-side: Middleware or edge function assigns variant (no layout shift). Client-side: Load variant after page load (can cause flicker). Implementation: Assign user to bucket (hash of user ID), store in cookie, serve variant. Metrics: Track conversion rate per variant. Tools: LaunchDarkly, Optimizely, GrowthBook, or custom with feature flags.

qa.question: What is image optimization strategy?
qa.answerHtml: Format: AVIF (best compression) > WebP > JPEG. Use <picture> for format fallbacks. Sizing: srcset with multiple widths, sizes attribute for viewport breakpoints. Loading: loading='lazy' for below-fold, fetchpriority='high' for hero. Placeholder: blur-up, LQIP, or solid color. CDN: Image CDN (Cloudflare Images, Imgix) for on-demand resizing. Next.js Image component handles most of this.

qa.question: How do you implement infinite scroll?
qa.answerHtml: Trigger: Intersection Observer on a sentinel element at the bottom. Data: Cursor-based pagination (not offset-based — offset breaks with new items). Loading: TanStack Query's useInfiniteQuery manages pages, loading states, error recovery. Performance: Virtualize rendered items (@tanstack/react-virtual). UX: Loading spinner at bottom, 'load more' button as fallback, scroll restoration on back navigation.

qa.question: Design a notification system.
qa.answerHtml: Delivery: WebSocket for real-time, Server-Sent Events for simpler setup, or polling as fallback. Types: In-app (toast/badge), push (Service Worker + Push API), email (server-side). State: Unread count (badge), notification list (paginated), read/unread toggle. Storage: Server database for history, client cache (TanStack Query). UX: Toast for new notifications, bell icon with unread count, notification center panel. Permission: Request push permission after user engagement (not on first visit).

qa.question: Design a rich text editor (Medium-like).
qa.answerHtml: Foundation: contentEditable div (raw) or editor framework: ProseMirror (most flexible), TipTap (React-friendly wrapper), Lexical (Meta's, React-first), Slate.js. Data model: Document as AST (not HTML string) — enables operations, collaboration, validation. Features: Toolbar (bold, italic, headings, links), slash commands, image upload (presigned S3 URLs), embed (paste URL → preview). Collaboration: Yjs CRDT integration. Output: Serialize to HTML or custom format for rendering.

qa.question: How do you handle feature flags in frontend?
qa.answerHtml: Architecture: Feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, custom). Flags evaluated server-side (no flash of wrong content) or client-side (more flexible). Implementation: Context provider wrapping app, useFeatureFlag('new-checkout') hook. Server Components: Check flags in Server Components (no client JS for flag evaluation). Types: Boolean (on/off), percentage rollout, user targeting (beta users), A/B variants. Cleanup: Remove old flags regularly — feature flag debt is real.

qa.question: Design a search experience (Google-like).
qa.answerHtml: Input: Debounced search input (300ms), autocomplete suggestions (separate API). Results: Server-rendered for SEO, paginated or infinite scroll, highlighted matching terms. Filters: URL search params for shareable/bookmarkable state. Performance: Search suggestions cached, results cached with SWR pattern. UX: Keyboard navigation in suggestions, recent searches (localStorage), no-results state with suggestions. Advanced: Faceted search, typo tolerance (server-side), search analytics.

qa.question: What are the trade-offs of SSR vs SSG vs ISR?
qa.answerHtml: SSG: Fastest delivery (CDN), can't personalize, stale until rebuild. Best: blog, docs, marketing. SSR: Always fresh, personalized, server load per request, slower TTFB. Best: dashboard, auth pages. ISR: CDN speed + periodic freshness, stale content possible for brief period. Best: e-commerce, CMS. PPR: Combines static shell with dynamic parts, best of all worlds but most complex. Choose based on: content freshness requirements, personalization needs, traffic volume.

qa.question: How would you design an offline-first application?
qa.answerHtml: Service Worker: Cache-first strategy for static assets, network-first for API data. Storage: IndexedDB for structured data, Cache API for responses. Sync: Background Sync API for pending mutations when back online. Conflict resolution: Last-write-wins (simple) or CRDT (complex but correct). UX: Show cached data with 'offline' indicator, queue mutations with 'pending' state, sync indicator when back online. Libraries: Workbox for Service Worker, Dexie for IndexedDB.

qa.question: Design an analytics dashboard.
qa.answerHtml: Data: Server Components fetch aggregated data (not raw events). Multiple parallel data fetches with Promise.all. Rendering: PPR — static layout/navigation, dynamic charts/metrics. Charts: Recharts, Chart.js, or D3 for complex visualizations. Lazy-load chart library. Interactivity: Date range picker, filters as URL params, drill-down navigation. Real-time: SSE or polling for live metrics. Performance: Virtual table for large datasets, skeleton loading for charts, memoize expensive calculations. Export: CSV/PDF generation via Server Actions.
